mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophy's core branches (epistemology, ethics, logic, metaphysics) and additional subfields (aesthetics, philosophy of language, mind, religion, science) directly map to diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1) and the divisions of philosophy (B) including metaphysics (B.1), epistemology (B.3), ethics (B.6), and aesthetics (B.8).

Epistemology examining propositional knowledge, justification, and the sources of knowledge (perception, reason, testimony) directly maps to the theory of knowledge (B.3), its history, nature, and scope (B.3.a), its relation to metaphysics and logic (B.3.b), and problems in epistemology including skepticism, foundationalism, and coherentism (B.3.c).

Aristotle's systematic works on logic, metaphysics (substance, potentiality, actuality), ethics, and politics connect to diverse views of the methods of philosophy (A.2), forms of philosophical exposition including systematically ordered treatises (A.3), and multiple divisions including metaphysics (B.1), epistemology (B.3), philosophy of mind through psychology (B.4), and ethics (B.6).

Kant's Critique of Pure Reason addresses metaphysics and its limits (B.1), Critique of Practical Reason addresses ethics through the categorical imperative (B.6), Critique of Judgment addresses aesthetics and teleology (B.8), his political philosophy connects to B.7, and his critical examination of reason is central to epistemology (B.3).
Time as a fundamental dimension of spacetime with philosophical tensions about its irreversibility connects to problems in metaphysics (B.1.c) and the philosophy of physics (B.2.d).

Reason as the cognitive capacity for logical processing, with tensions between reason and faith and between substantive and formal reason, connects to diverse views of methods of philosophy (A.2), criteria of meaning and truth (A.4), epistemology (B.3), and the philosophy of mind (B.4).

Cognitive processes including Platonism, Aristotelianism, computationalism, and phenomenology directly map to the philosophy of mind (B.4), its history and scope (B.4.a), and problems in philosophy of mind including the nature of thought and consciousness (B.4.c).

The mind-body problem, physicalist resolution, and the attribution of mind to non-human agents directly map to the philosophy of mind (B.4), its history and scope (B.4.a), its relation to empirical and mathematical sciences (B.4.b), and problems in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c).

Descartes' method of radical doubt represents a view of philosophical method (A.2), mind-body dualism connects to metaphysics (B.1) and philosophy of mind (B.4), and his foundationalism is central to epistemology (B.3).
Mathematical proof establishing truth through axioms and inference rules connects to the philosophy of mathematics (B.11).

Ethics covering normative ethics (consequentialism, deontology, virtue ethics), metaethics, and applied ethics directly maps to ethics or moral philosophy (B.6), the distinction between metaethics and normative ethics (B.6.a), its relation to other philosophical disciplines (B.6.b), and problems in ethics (B.6.c).

Socrates' dialectical method (elenchus) represents a distinctive view of philosophical method (A.2), and his ethical concerns with justice, virtue, and piety connect to ethics or moral philosophy (B.6).

Plato's philosophical dialogues represent a form of philosophical exposition (A.3), his Theory of Forms connects to metaphysics (B.1), his epistemology to theory of knowledge (B.3), and his Republic's advocacy of philosopher-kings to political philosophy (B.7).

Aquinas' natural theology and proofs for God's existence connect to metaphysics and its relation to natural theology (B.1.b), his moral philosophy connects to ethics (B.6), and his faith-reason reconciliation connects to philosophy of religion (B.14).

The Enlightenment's emphasis on empirical inquiry and reason connects to epistemology (B.3), its political theory of natural rights and constitutional government to political philosophy (B.7), and its focus on science and natural history to philosophy of science (B.13).
The moral dichotomy of good and evil with contested ontological status (force, privation, social construct) connects to problems in metaphysics (B.1.c) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Knowledge as true justified belief, with Gettier problem challenges and foundationalism vs. coherentism debates, directly maps to epistemology or theory of knowledge (B.3) and its problems (B.3.c).
Machiavelli's analysis of political power, The Prince, and civic republicanism connect to political philosophy (B.7) and its history (B.7.c).

Freud's theories of the unconscious, repression, and the structural model of the mind (id, ego, superego) connect to the philosophy of mind (B.4), and his cultural analysis connects to the philosophy of man or philosophical anthropology (B.5).
Gödel's incompleteness theorems setting permanent limits on mathematical provability connect to the philosophy of logic (B.10) and philosophy of mathematics (B.11).
The history of philosophy surveying diverse philosophical traditions connects to diverse conceptions of philosophy (A.1) and provides historical context for all divisions of philosophy (B).

Aesthetics examining beauty, taste, and art directly maps to the nature and scope of aesthetics (B.8.a), its development from antiquity through 18th-century emergence (B.8.b), problems in aesthetics including subjectivism vs. objectivism (B.8.c), and its relation to ethics, psychology, and comparative traditions (B.8.d).
Art's contested definition, with the philosophy of art (aesthetics) examining creativity and interpretation, connects to aesthetics (B.8) and the philosophy of art (B.12).

Metaphysics examining being, existence, ontology, causation, and modality directly maps to metaphysics or speculative philosophy (B.1), its history, nature, and scope (B.1.a), its relation to ethics and logic (B.1.b), and problems in metaphysics (B.1.c).
Eastern philosophical traditions including Confucianism, Taoism, Vedanta, and Buddhism represent diverse conceptions of philosophy distinct from Western approaches (A.1).
Political science as the empirical study of governance connects to the nature and scope of political philosophy and its relation to political science (B.7.a).
Mathematics covering pure and applied domains, the axiomatic method, and foundational questions connects to the philosophy of mathematics (B.11).
Logic as the study of correct reasoning from Aristotelian syllogistics through Frege connects to the philosophy of logic (B.10).

Psychology as the scientific study of mind and behavior connects to the philosophy of mind's relation to empirical sciences (B.4.b) and philosophical anthropology's relation to social sciences (B.5.b).
Truth theories (correspondence, coherence, pragmatism, deflationary) connect to criteria of meaning and truth in philosophical thought (A.4) and problems in epistemology (B.3.c).

Ontology as the study of being, existence, and the most general categories of reality maps to metaphysics (B.1), its nature and scope (B.1.a), and problems including universals vs. particulars and realism vs. anti-realism (B.1.c).
Education's aims, learning theories, and history from ancient literacy to modern schooling connect to the philosophy of education (B.16).
Avicenna's Book of Healing covering metaphysics and logic connects to metaphysics (B.1), and his philosophical psychology connects to philosophy of mind (B.4).

The scientific method with its empirical framework, falsifiability, and philosophical tensions between empiricism and rationalism connects to the philosophy of nature and its relation to science (B.2.b) and the philosophy of science (B.13).

The concept of nature spanning physical world phenomena with philosophical tensions about the nature/culture divide connects to the philosophy of nature (B.2), its history and scope (B.2.a), and the basic aspects of the natural order (B.2.c).
Human rights as universally recognized moral principles grounded in natural rights theory, with tensions between universalism and cultural relativism, connect to political philosophy (B.7).

Cicero's adaptation of Hellenistic philosophical schools into Latin treatises represents a form of philosophical exposition through commentaries (A.3), and his works connect to ethics (B.6) and political philosophy through constitutional law (B.7).
Locke's tabula rasa and experiential origin of ideas connect to epistemology (B.3), and his natural rights, social contract, and theory of government consent connect to political philosophy (B.7).
The arts as encompassing visual, literary, and performing arts with aesthetic theory connect to aesthetics (B.8) and the philosophy of art (B.12).
Emotion as studied across psychology and philosophy, with somatic and cognitive theories, connects to problems in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c).
Galileo's experimental method and championing of heliocentrism connect to the philosophy of nature (B.2) and the philosophy of science through the development of modern scientific methodology (B.13).
Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection connects to the philosophy of biology (B.2.e) and the philosophy of science through its implications for teleology and design arguments (B.13).
Property rights theories from Locke's labor theory through Marx's critique connect to political philosophy (B.7).
Social science disciplines studying human societies connect to the relation of philosophical anthropology to social sciences (B.5.b).

Anthropology's study of humanity encompassing biology, society, and culture connects to the philosophy of man or philosophical anthropology (B.5) and its relation to physical and cultural anthropology (B.5.b).
